pattern: Treat every non-prime, same-length number as a node and each single-digit nudge as an edge weighted by the number you land on, then run a shortest-path search from n to m.
hints: A single operation only ever changes one digit by one, so picture every reachable number as a node connected to its digit-neighbors.

The running cost of a path is the sum of every number visited along it, which is exactly what a weighted shortest-path search accumulates if each edge is charged the value of the number it arrives at.
Numbers that are prime can never be entered or started from -- rule those out before searching, including both endpoints up front.

edge_inputs.description: n already equals m and neither is prime
the target itself is prime, making the task impossible
the starting value itself is prime, making the task impossible
four-digit values near the top of the allowed range
same digit count but requires several digits to change in both directions

stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    d = 4
    lo = 10 ** (d - 1)
    hi = 10 ** d - 1
    n = rng.randint(lo, hi)
    m = rng.randint(lo, hi)
    return [n, m]
